speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 2. Today is October 23, 1996. And we're interviewing Estelle Laughlin. I had a quick question for you. And then I'm going to bring you back to the incident you were about to tell me. What languages did you just speak when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish and Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you were about to tell me about an incident--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when we changed tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, when the news about the war was so much in the foreground, of course, it was also reflected in children's plays. And, and my friend, Janka-- I remember we were sitting on the parapet near the window. And we were-- we crossed our fingers. And we said, oh, wouldn't it be wonderful if there was war. It would be so exciting. And, and we wished for war. And only a week later, the bombing started. And we were-- and a whole-- like, a few families gathered in the same place. And we were in her apartment. And I, I, I, I remember sitting on the piano. And she looked at me. And I looked at her. And of course we felt that it was all our fault, that we brought it on. [LAUGHS] And then the bombings, the the infernal bombings started. Most of Poland surrendered very quickly. But Warsaw was defended for four weeks. They held the German invaders back for four weeks. And every night, continuous bombings. The light would just-- the sky would just be lit up with the rockets. And, and, and, and bombs would just shower on us. And it was so terrifying.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never knew if the-- if the bomb-- when you heard very often that whistle that precedes the explosion, you just sat there breathless and thinking, oh, am I going to be the target this time? And then you looked out the windows. And you saw the, the, the, the flames reaching the skies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And afterwards, of course-- after it would subside, you would go out. And you would see that what was there before wasn't anymore. You never knew if your neighbors, if your family who did not live very close to you-- whether they survived, whether they were all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a very frightening-- a very, very overwhelming [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and destructive. The houses just, just crumbled like cookies. It, it was awful. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after the-- oh, and during that time, the-- there was-- we, we had radio communication. And, of course, what you heard during the bombing was from the Polish government. And, of course, their reports reflected propaganda. And, and, and they kind of kept you informed about what was going on. But you had quite a few Volksdeutsche, which is Poles who-- whose ancestors were Germans. And they identified-- much more, or many-- identified with-- they saw themselves as Germans. But they were educated in Polish schools. And they spoke the language. They were Polish citizens. And they took over the radio. We-- well, we didn't know that. And one night, we hear that broadcast on the radio. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a broadcast. We did not hear it. But apparently enough people did hear the broadcasting. And, in the middle of the night, the gate was rattling. And my aunt and my uncle came pleading with my father. They said, you must-- you must leave. Because-- and then we turned on the radio. And then we heard. And the-- it got-- they-- the voice that came through on the radio identified themselves as Poles and said that the President [NON-ENGLISH]-- he was saying, in his name, all Polish young-- all Polish men lay down your, your guns and move towards Russia. Run away. Because when the Germans come and every single man is, is killed-- they just slaughter indiscriminately. Well, they did that to cause panic. Well, my father left. And we just felt so devastated. My mother-- and we were just so devastated. And I recall we just-- we couldn't-- my mother-- we could not bear staying home and be reminded of my father and that his presence wasn't there. So we went to that same aunt, all cousins and, and, and, and aunts-- we-- and my mother, my sister, and I. And one night-- and one morning, I woke up feeling very strange. I had this dream that my father sat down on my bed. Then it was my cousin that sat down on my bed. And I had such a strange feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she noticed that. And she said, what's the matter? And I said, I just felt my father coming home. And of course, she tried to soothe me. I was inconsolable that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember holding onto a box of chess-- a chess game. And I was sitting-- my aunt lived on the first-- on the ground floor. And I was sitting with this-- clutching this box all day, sitting at the window. And I had this very, very-- feeling-- overwhelming feeling of my father's presence. It was like a premonition. I don't believe in it. [LAUGHS] But all I can-- but, you know, it happened. And, you know, between raids, children are children. And between raids, the children would just float out of their apartments. And they would play and, and, and, and just-- led the life, the robustness, the laughter, the joy. And they called. And they called. I said, I cannot go out and play. And that day, my father did come home. I was just sitting there at the window. And he returned. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's one recollection again that kind of has this balance of, of deep bitterness and deep pain and deep joy. And my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, shortly after, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the resistance, the, the defense of Poland-- of Warsaw crumbled. See, all I've told you up, up until now, as bad as it was, was only a prelude [LAUGHS] of, of the darkness that really descended. Even that has, has, has, has moments that glow. After that, things got much, much, dimmer, much, much worse. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember the Germans marching in. It was like a human, enormous machine. They wore leather-- you, you, you, you forget-- I-- they, they didn't even seem human because their faces were so immobile, so rigid. So-- it's as though-- there, there, there was not a flicker of what I associate with a human face-- a blink, a smile, nothing. They just marched, helmets and leather and, and, and guns and bayonets and boots. And we-- the, the, the rasping, the beating of the march on, on, on, on the cobblestones, it just rang. It just rang. It, it, it just was like-- it, it filled the air. It just filled the air. And, of course, for us, we didn't know-- we didn't know what to expect. I, as a child-- it was just overwhelmed by the fear, by, by, by, by the, I guess, inhumanity of it. It just, just, just did not match with the world that I have known. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They marched in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, then the-- of course, you didn't realize that the Germans were not stationed in the areas where we lived. They were pretty much-- I don't know where. But they were outside where, where, where we lived. But they did come in. They came in always unexpectedly, deliberately because they did want to catch us off guard. One of the first things that I recall is confiscations. We had to surrender all the gold, wedding bands, and, and, and everything of value. You know, it was not the material value that, that-- the material loss that really counted that much, except that it's an invasion. It's just-- it, it, it, it was robbing. It was plunder. I remember as a child asking my mother that I remembered that so often people would say Jews are greedy. And here, the people come into my house. They steal from me. They plunder from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they call me greedy? I mean, why did-- why did they take their sons and their husbands and their daughters and send them into war if it was not for greed, if it was not for plunder? And it just absolutely confused me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to surrender things of value-- of value materially, but more above that, of sentimental value. The-- I, I don't recall whether the, the water was shut off. But I do recall distinctly that it might have been that the pipes were broken. I don't remember. But it seems to me that the water was shut off. And I remember that we had to walk a certain distance with buckets. And, you know, [LAUGHS] it was not as though we could go to stores, that the stores were prepared with merchandise that we needed. And so we had to be very sparing with the water. I remember we had to go with the buckets for water during the winter where it was slippery and icy. They shut the lights off. And we-- and, and, and of course, there were blackouts. And we used carbide lights. And books were confiscated. And schools were shut. And yet, you know, when people talk about the resistance, I associate resistance with our existence, with our attempt to remain human, with our attempt to preserve our humanity, with holding on to our values. To me, that was a resistance. Because when we went to-- we, we had hidden schools. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also-- before I, I, I, I will share with you about the hidden schools, I would like to also list some of the other things that occurred that come to mind. There were rations. There were armbands. We had to wear a, a, a, a, a band with-- on, on, on our arm always, always. And as children, we must not forget-- with the Star of David. I think there was pride in the Star of David, gave us the identification. But not the way-- not-- it, it, it was humiliating to use it as, as, as, as, as, as a print like you put on cattle, like a number on a-- on cattle. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The, the, the, the first-- the Jewish community really organized itself very quickly. There were committees formed. There were committees formed in every building. There was the Jewish committee, like a Jewish Community Center. That was like a Jewish government, which, in some ways, the Germans supported because it was their way of, of communicating with the-- with the Jewish community. Very often, the Germans would pass on mandates to the Jewish community. And they had to carry it out. And I don't recall the name of the president of the Jewish community. I, I hope that I recall during the interview, because he was a very decent person. And later on, when the people-- when they started to send people to extermination camps, he had-- at some point, he really had no choice but to give names. But at the end, he shot himself. He just could not do it. I am not sure if he surrendered any names. But I do know that, for instance, conveying to us that we have to surrender-- give up our possessions were, I think-- I think, communicated-- these ordinances were communicated. The degree to which we cooperated was very often to protect the community. There was-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't mean to interrupt you, but I-- before you go more into what I guess became a Judenrat of some kind--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was-- what were some of the first things that the Germans themselves did to the Jews? Did you witness any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --ill treatment of the Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at the hands of the Germans directly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, don't let me forget to share about the schools, the secret schools. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans were not really, as I said, physically always in the ghetto. But they did make-- they did appear. Even when it was quiet, it was an eerie quiet. You always heard gunshots in the distance. You always were aware of their presence. You, you heard wailings. You had-- maybe suddenly you would hear the wailing of, of, of, of a mother, of a father, of a family because somebody was shot. And they just found out. Or you would sometimes see them where they would just come into, like, our courtyard or our street. We had a neighbor. Their name was Frankel. And he was a Hasidic-- he was a Hasid. The whole family was a very learned family. They spoke many languages. They were very, very cultured and very, very charitable. To them, religion was a brotherhood, a way of, of, of taking care of people. Every Friday, at his table you had-- they always, always on Friday, on Shabbat, there were always numerous people who either were poor-- it didn't matter. It was just-- the house was just open. And, and they were-- and their children were our friends. Well, he was such a handsome, stately, tall man with this-- looks so reverent. And with this beard-- a young man. [CRYING] They ripped his beard out. They beat him. They kick him. [CRYING] I mean, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, what-- that it would stop-- it would, would seem to-- you know, memory is selective. And I'm glad that I had the good fortune of having these memories available to me. I don't [LAUGHS] mean the bad ones. But I mean the kindness, the warmth, and, and, and the security of my community-- and the joy and the singing and the love for nature. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm glad that this was available to me. And I hope that this is available to my progeny, too. I like to share. [LAUGHS] I always like to share the good-- the good part. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, there, there, there, there were-- there was one neighbor in our building-- it was a four-story building. And we had balconies. And he just went crazy. And I-- almost every morning-- I don't know if it was every morning-- and I don't-- he, he would just pierce the silence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we always tried to be very inconspicuous, very unseen, very unheard, not, not to be heard. You don't want to provoke. And I just went crazy. I think that he lost his son. He was just gobbled up. He just screamed. He just wailed and just screamed every morning. And it was-- it, it was-- it was a serious concern because there was a danger of, of, of attracting attention. And there was also such a feeling of protecting him because we knew he's suffering. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Another, another thing-- another thing that that the Jewish-- that in our immediate environment, in our-- in our building, there was a committee formed. And kitchens were established. Because, you see, Warsaw became a [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a-- it's a, a death basket-- box. The Jews from the surrounding areas-- they scrubbed the areas clean of Jews. And, and, and, and they brought them all into Warsaw. And, and, and Warsaw was just littered with hungry people. Cold-- they died of cold. They died from hunger. And literally, when we walked out of our house, there were piles, piles of rotting corpses, murmuring corpses. Because not all-- not, you know, there were some who still held on to the breath of life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was awful. There was-- I mean, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these pictures just flash back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a, a, a, a little boy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a genius. He was a-- there were lots of beggars. And, you know, anybody turned into a-- lots of people turned into beggars. And he, he played the violin so beautifully. And I remember how everyone-- you know, there wasn't only that, that pity because the sensitive little boy standing there. He may have been, maybe, 10 years old, maybe a little older, maybe not. But everyone was also so touched by the-- by the beauty of his playing. He was giving. And everybody just received it. And everybody shared what they could share-- a piece of their ration bread, or whatever. But there was sharing. My father hardly ever left the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He, he was-- he was-- he, he-- but he supported the theater. You know, there was-- there, there, there were theaters in Warsaw. There were hidden library-- books. Where my-- that's all I remember my father spent a great deal of time of was just sitting near the stove when we had wood and, and, and just reading. He lost a lot of weight. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Well, there were theaters. And we supported the theaters. Also, a lot of poetry was written during the war. My sister-- I will share the book with you later-- wrote a book about-- she collected the poetry written in-- during the war in Warsaw Ghetto, both in Yiddish and in Polish, and translated them and added commentaries about them to set them into the-- into the setting, into the framework of the times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I'll come back now. There were theaters. And, and, and, and, and now I will come back to, to, to the schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the very early schools, where we called them [NON-ENGLISH]-- and that means 'gardens.' The first attempt-- the first direction that the community focused on is save the children. At that time, at the very beginning, saving the children meant saving the best in them, saving the most humane in them. And so, little schools that did not have exactly-- there wasn't that much emphasis on, on instructions as it was to let the children play. Let, let the children sing. Let the children feel, feel the air of freedom. And let them be children. Then-- that stopped very, very quickly. I, I don't know whether it was because the funds ran out. I doubt that very much. I think, and I am not certain, that anything that was organized was suspicious. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just don't know why they eventually disappeared. I guess there were funds missing or lacking. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to pause to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, OK.
